event: Coronation of the Virgin by the Holy Trinity
symbols: crown placed on Mary's head — her exaltation as Queen of Heaven
two divine figures crowning Mary — God the Father and God the Son acting in unity
dove between them — the Holy Spirit completing the Trinitarian action
kneeling Virgin — humility exalted, the handmaid made queen
clouds and golden light — the heavenly court and divine glory
putti/angels — the heavenly hosts witnessing the coronation
blue robes on the divine figures — heavenly authority and divinity
description: A Baroque painting showing the Coronation of the Virgin Mary by the Holy Trinity. Mary kneels at the center with her head bowed and hands clasped in prayer, wearing a reddish-pink robe. Two male figures in blue robes — God the Father (with white hair) and Christ — stand on either side, jointly placing a crown on her head. Between and above them, a white dove representing the Holy Spirit descends in a blaze of golden light. Putti and cherubs float in the clouds around the scene. The composition is dramatically lit from behind the dove, creating a radiant, heavenly atmosphere.
theological_description: The Coronation of the Virgin by the Holy Trinity represents the culmination of Marian theology: the humble handmaid who said 'let it be to me according to your word' (Luke 1:38) is now crowned Queen of Heaven by the triune God. The Magnificat's prophecy is fulfilled: 'He has exalted those of humble estate' (Luke 1:52). The Trinitarian coronation — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit acting in concert — affirms that Mary's glorification is the work of the entire Godhead. The scene draws from the imagery of Psalm 45:9: 'At your right hand stands the queen in gold of Ophir,' and Revelation 12:1: 'A woman clothed with the sun, with the moon under her feet, and on her head a crown of twelve stars.' Mary's kneeling posture even in glorification embodies the paradox Jesus taught: 'Whoever humbles himself will be exalted' (Matthew 23:12). The dove of the Holy Spirit, who overshadowed Mary at the Incarnation (Luke 1:35), now completes the arc by participating in her coronation. This image teaches that faithfulness to God's will, however costly, leads to unimaginable glory — 'what God has prepared for those who love him' (1 Corinthians 2:9).

scripture_refs.reason: All generations will call me blessed; he has exalted the humble — the Magnificat fulfilled in Mary's coronation
The woman crowned with stars — the scriptural image underlying the Coronation of the Virgin
At your right hand stands the queen in gold — the royal psalm applied to Mary's queenship
Whoever humbles himself will be exalted — the theological principle of Mary's coronation
The Holy Spirit will come upon you — the Spirit who initiated the Incarnation now crowns its fruit
